subject: ap-chemistry
title: CuSO₄ Analysis: Precipitation & Spectrophotometry
stimulus:
A student is given the task of determining the molar concentration of a CuSO₄ solution using two different procedures: precipitation and spectrophotometry.

For the precipitation experiment, 20.0 mL of 0.200 M Ba(NO₃)₂ is added to 50.0 mL of the CuSO₄(aq). The reaction goes to completion and a white precipitate forms. Data: mass of dry filter paper = 0.764 g; mass of filter paper + dried precipitate = 1.136 g.

parts.prompt: Write a balanced net ionic equation for the precipitation reaction.
parts.scoring_notes: Ba²⁺(aq) + SO₄²⁻(aq) → BaSO₄(s). Ba(NO₃)₂ and CuSO₄ are soluble ionic compounds; NO₃⁻ and Cu²⁺ are spectator ions. BaSO₄ is the insoluble precipitate (white solid). Net ionic equation shows only species that change.

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced net ionic equation Ba²⁺(aq) + SO₄²⁻(aq) → BaSO₄(s): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$\text{Ba}^{2+}(aq) + \text{SO}_4^{2-}(aq) \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4(s)$

Spectator ions (Cu²⁺ and NO₃⁻) are omitted. BaSO₄ is insoluble (white precipitate). Charge: 2+ + 2− = 0 on left; 0 on right ✓

wrong_examples: Including Cu²⁺ and NO₃⁻ in the net ionic equation — these are spectator ions and must be omitted.
Writing Ba(NO₃)₂ + CuSO₄ → BaSO₄ + Cu(NO₃)₂ as the net ionic equation — this is the molecular equation, not the net ionic.
common_traps: Net ionic equation: write only the ions that form the precipitate. Spectator ions (same on both sides) are omitted. BaSO₄ is insoluble (white precipitate); CuSO₄ and Ba(NO₃)₂ are soluble.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct balanced net ionic equation: 1 point. $\text{Ba}^{2+}(aq) + \text{SO}_4^{2-}(aq) \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4(s)$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct net ionic equation; spectator ions $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ and $\text{NO}_3^-$ properly omitted.
This is the molecular equation, not the net ionic equation.

parts.prompt: Calculate the number of moles of precipitate formed.
parts.scoring_notes: Mass of precipitate = 1.136 − 0.764 = 0.372 g BaSO₄. Molar mass of BaSO₄ = 137.33 + 32.06 + 4(16.00) = 233.39 g/mol. Moles BaSO₄ = 0.372 g / 233.39 g/mol = 1.59×10⁻³ mol.

scoring_points.description: For the correct mass of precipitate = 0.372 g (may be implicit): 1 point

required_elements: Mass of BaSO₄ = 1.136 g − 0.764 g = 0.372 g
alternatives.correct_example:
Mass of BaSO₄ = mass(filter paper + precipitate) − mass(filter paper alone)
= 1.136 g − 0.764 g = 0.372 g

wrong_examples: Using 1.136 g as the mass of precipitate — must subtract the filter paper mass.
Using 0.764 g as the precipitate mass — that is the filter paper alone.
common_traps: Mass of precipitate = total mass − filter paper mass = 1.136 − 0.764 = 0.372 g. Always subtract the tare (filter paper alone) from the gross mass.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value of the mass of precipitate (may be implicit): 1 point. $1.136\text{ g} - 0.764\text{ g} = 0.372\text{ g BaSO}_4$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct subtraction of filter paper tare from total mass.
Did not subtract filter paper mass; 1.136 g includes the filter paper.

scoring_points.description: For the correct moles of BaSO₄ consistent with mass from b1: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using molar mass of Ba alone (137.33 g/mol) or BaO — must use molar mass of the precipitate BaSO₄ (233.39 g/mol).
Not dividing mass by molar mass — must convert grams to moles using M = m/n.
common_traps: Moles = mass / molar mass. BaSO₄ molar mass = 137.33 + 32.06 + 4(16.00) = 233.39 g/mol. Accept value consistent with student's mass from b1.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value of the number of moles, consistent with mass of precipitate: 1 point. $0.372\text{ g} \times \frac{1\text{ mol}}{233.39\text{ g}} = 0.00159\text{ mol}$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct molar mass and division; consistent with SG calculation.
Used molar mass of Ba alone instead of $\text{BaSO}_4$ (233.39 g/mol).

parts.prompt: Calculate the molarity of the original CuSO₄ solution.
parts.scoring_notes: Mole ratio: Ba²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → BaSO₄, so 1 mol BaSO₄ = 1 mol CuSO₄ = 1 mol SO₄²⁻. Moles CuSO₄ = moles BaSO₄ = 1.59×10⁻³ mol. Volume CuSO₄ = 50.0 mL = 0.0500 L. [CuSO₄] = 1.59×10⁻³ mol / 0.0500 L = 0.0318 M.

scoring_points.description: For the correct [CuSO₄] ≈ 0.0318 M consistent with part (b): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
From net ionic equation: 1 mol BaSO₄ produced per 1 mol CuSO₄.
$n(\text{CuSO}_4) = n(\text{BaSO}_4) = 1.594 \times 10^{-3}$ mol
$[\text{CuSO}_4] = \dfrac{1.594 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}}{0.0500 \text{ L}} = 0.0319$ M

wrong_examples: Using volume = (20.0 + 50.0) mL = 70.0 mL — the question asks for the concentration of the original CuSO₄ solution, which had volume 50.0 mL.
Using 1:2 mole ratio — the net ionic equation shows 1:1 Ba²⁺ to SO₄²⁻, and CuSO₄ provides 1 mol SO₄²⁻ per mol.
common_traps: M = n/V. Use original volume of CuSO₄ (50.0 mL = 0.0500 L), not total mixed volume. Mole ratio from net ionic equation: 1 mol BaSO₄ ≡ 1 mol CuSO₄. Answer should be consistent with b2.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value, consistent with part (b): 1 point. $[\text{CuSO}_4] = 0.00159\text{ mol} / 0.0500\text{ L} = 0.0318$ M.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly uses original 50.0 mL CuSO₄ volume and 1:1 mole ratio with BaSO₄.
Used total mixed volume (70.0 mL) rather than original CuSO₄ volume (50.0 mL).

parts.prompt: Calculate the volume of 0.1000 M CuSO₄(aq) needed to make 50.00 mL of 0.0500 M CuSO₄(aq).

scoring_points.description: For the correct volume = 25.0 mL using M₁V₁ = M₂V₂: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
Dilution: $M_1 V_1 = M_2 V_2$
$(0.1000 \text{ M})(V_1) = (0.0500 \text{ M})(50.00 \text{ mL})$
$V_1 = 25.0 \text{ mL}$ of 0.1000 M CuSO₄ needed

wrong_examples: Using M₁/V₁ = M₂/V₂ — the correct dilution equation is M₁V₁ = M₂V₂ (moles are conserved).
Saying 50.0 mL — that is the final volume, not the volume of concentrated stock needed.
common_traps: Dilution: M₁V₁ = M₂V₂. More concentrated (0.1000 M) × smaller volume = less concentrated (0.0500 M) × larger volume. Units for V can be mL as long as consistent on both sides.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value: 1 point. $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$; $V_1 = (0.0500)(50.00)/(0.1000) = 25.0$ mL.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct dilution equation applied; answer is exactly half the final volume as expected.

sample_responses.response: 25.0 mL of water added to 25.0 mL of stock
sample_responses.commentary: Adding 25.0 mL water to 25.0 mL stock gives 50.0 mL total but student must specify the volume of stock (not water) — this answer is actually correct if interpreted properly, but requires explicit identification of stock volume.

parts.prompt: Briefly describe the procedure the student should follow to make 50.00 mL of 0.0500 M CuSO₄(aq) using 0.1000 M CuSO₄(aq), a 50.00 mL volumetric flask, and other standard laboratory equipment.
parts.scoring_notes: Step 1: Measure 25.0 mL of 0.1000 M CuSO₄ using a volumetric pipette (or graduated pipette). Step 2: Transfer the 25.0 mL to a 50.00 mL volumetric flask. Step 3: Add distilled/deionized water to the flask until the volume reaches exactly the 50.00 mL mark (stopper and mix). Key points: use pipette (not graduated cylinder) for accurate volume; add water to the mark on the volumetric flask.

scoring_points.description: For a correct technique to accurately measure the 25.0 mL of stock solution: 1 point

required_elements: Use a volumetric pipette (or buret) to measure exactly 25.0 mL of 0.1000 M CuSO₄.
alternatives.correct_example: Use a volumetric (or graduated) pipette to measure 25.0 mL of the 0.1000 M CuSO₄ solution and transfer it to the 50.00 mL volumetric flask.

wrong_examples: Using a beaker to measure 25.0 mL — beakers are not volumetric instruments and cannot deliver accurate volumes.
Not specifying the volume (just saying 'add some') — must specify 25.0 mL.
common_traps: For accurate volume delivery: use a volumetric pipette or buret. Graduated cylinders are acceptable but less precise. Beakers and flasks (other than volumetric flasks) are NOT acceptable for measuring volume.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For a correct technique to measure the volume of solution: 1 point. Measure out the correct volume (25.0 mL) of 0.1000 M $\text{CuSO}_4$ with a volumetric pipet (graduated cylinder or buret also acceptable).

sample_responses.response: Use a 25.0 mL volumetric pipet to measure exactly 25.0 mL of the 0.1000 M $\text{CuSO}_4$ solution.
sample_responses.commentary: Matches SG accepted technique; volumetric pipet specified for precise volume.

sample_responses.response: Use a beaker to pour approximately 25 mL of stock solution.
sample_responses.commentary: Beakers are not volumetric instruments and cannot deliver accurate volumes.

scoring_points.description: For correctly diluting to the 50.00 mL mark in the volumetric flask: 1 point

required_elements: Transfer to the 50.00 mL volumetric flask and add distilled water until the meniscus reaches exactly the 50.00 mL calibration mark.
alternatives.correct_example: Transfer the 25.0 mL of CuSO₄ solution to the 50.00 mL volumetric flask. Add distilled water carefully until the bottom of the meniscus is exactly at the 50.00 mL mark. Stopper the flask and invert several times to mix thoroughly.

wrong_examples: Saying 'add 25.0 mL of water' — must dilute to the calibration mark, not add a fixed volume, because the stock solution itself contributes volume.
Using a graduated cylinder as the final container — the 50.00 mL volumetric flask has higher accuracy for making the solution.
common_traps: Volumetric flask: always fill to the calibration mark (not 'add X mL water'). The stock solution already occupies some volume, so adding exactly 25.0 mL water would give slightly more than 50.00 mL total.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For a correct technique to dilute the solution to the final volume: 1 point. Transfer the 25.0 mL to a 50.00 mL volumetric flask and dilute with water up to the 50.00 mL calibration mark.

sample_responses.response: Transfer the 25.0 mL of $\text{CuSO}_4$ solution to the 50.00 mL volumetric flask, add distilled water until the meniscus reaches exactly the 50.00 mL mark, and mix.
sample_responses.commentary: Correct dilution procedure; uses calibration mark rather than adding a fixed water volume.

sample_responses.response: Add 25.0 mL of distilled water to the stock solution.
sample_responses.commentary: The solution volume itself displaces some water volume; adding exactly 25.0 mL water overshoots the 50.00 mL mark.

parts.prompt: The absorbance of the CuSO₄ solution of unknown concentration is 0.219. Using the standard curve, determine the molarity of the solution.
parts.scoring_notes: Using Beer–Lambert law (A = εbc) and the standard curve: read the concentration corresponding to absorbance = 0.219. From the standard curve (linear fit through the known standards), absorbance 0.219 corresponds to approximately 0.035 M (accept 0.032–0.038 M based on reading the graph). Alternatively: slope of standard curve ≈ 6.3 AU/M (absorbance units per M). [CuSO₄] = A/slope = 0.219/6.3 ≈ 0.035 M.

scoring_points.description: For the correct concentration consistent with the standard curve (0.032–0.038 M): 1 point

required_elements: $[\text{CuSO}_4] \approx 0.035$ M (accept 0.032–0.038 M consistent with reading the standard curve at absorbance = 0.219)
alternatives.correct_example:
From the standard curve: at absorbance = 0.219, read the corresponding concentration on the x-axis.
Using the linear equation of the standard curve: A = 6.3[C] → [C] = 0.219/6.3 ≈ 0.035 M

wrong_examples: Reading the y-axis as concentration — the standard curve has absorbance on the y-axis and concentration on the x-axis; read horizontally to find concentration.
Using 0.219 M directly as the answer — absorbance is not the same as concentration; use the standard curve to convert.
common_traps: Beer–Lambert: A = εbc. Use the standard curve to convert absorbance (y) to concentration (x). At A = 0.219, trace horizontally to the line, then drop vertically to the x-axis. Accept any value consistent with reading the graph.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct value (between 0.032 M and 0.038 M): 1 point. Estimated value from graph at A = 0.219 gives approximately 0.035 M using slope ≈ 6.3 AU/M.

sample_responses.response: Using the standard curve: $[\text{CuSO}_4] = A/6.3 = 0.219/6.3 \approx 0.035$ M
sample_responses.commentary: Correct application of Beer–Lambert linear relationship; answer within accepted 0.032–0.038 M range.
Absorbance (dimensionless ratio) is not the same as concentration; the standard curve must be used to convert.

parts.prompt: A second student performs the same spectrophotometry experiment, but there are a few drops of water in the cuvette before the student adds the CuSO₄(aq) solution of unknown concentration. Will this result in a CuSO₄(aq) concentration for the unknown that is greater than, less than, or equal to the concentration determined in part (f)? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Less than. The drops of water dilute the CuSO₄ solution in the cuvette, reducing its actual concentration. A lower concentration means less Cu²⁺ to absorb light → lower absorbance measured. When the student reads the standard curve using this lower absorbance value, the reported concentration will be lower than the true value (less than the part f answer).

scoring_points.description: For correctly identifying the result as less than: 1 point

required_elements: Less than (the concentration determined will be less than in part f).
alternatives.correct_example: Less than. The water dilutes the CuSO₄ solution in the cuvette, making the actual concentration in the cuvette lower than the true unknown concentration.

wrong_examples: Saying greater than — water does not add Cu²⁺; it only dilutes the solution, reducing concentration.
Saying equal — adding water changes the concentration.
common_traps: Adding water to the cuvette dilutes the solution → lower [Cu²⁺] → lower absorbance → reading the standard curve at a lower A gives a lower concentration. Result: reported [CuSO₄] < true [CuSO₄].
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct answer: 1 point. The concentration will be less than that determined in part (f).

sample_responses.commentary: Correct answer; water dilutes the sample in the cuvette lowering absorbance and thus the reported concentration.
Water does not add $\text{Cu}^{2+}$; it only dilutes the solution, reducing absorbance.

scoring_points.description: For a correct justification involving lower concentration → lower absorbance → lower reported value: 1 point

required_elements: The water dilutes the CuSO₄ in the cuvette → lower actual [Cu²⁺] → lower absorbance measured → lower concentration read from the standard curve.
alternatives.correct_example: The drops of water dilute the CuSO₄ solution in the cuvette. A lower concentration of Cu²⁺ means less light is absorbed, giving a lower absorbance reading. When this lower absorbance is used with the standard curve, the concentration determined will be lower than the actual concentration of the original unknown solution.

wrong_examples: Saying the cuvette path length changes — adding drops of water to the cuvette does not affect the path length (the light still passes through the fixed cuvette width).
Mentioning only that the solution is diluted without connecting to lower absorbance → lower concentration reading.
common_traps: Chain of reasoning: extra water → diluted Cu²⁺ → fewer Cu²⁺ to absorb light → lower absorbance → standard curve gives lower concentration. Must connect dilution to the absorbance reading, not just say 'diluted.'
scoring_points.official_rubric: For a valid justification: 1 point. The additional water decreases the concentration of $\text{CuSO}_4$ in the cuvette, lowering the absorbance (Beer–Lambert), which results in a lower estimated concentration from the standard curve.

sample_responses.response: The drops of water dilute the $\text{CuSO}_4$ solution, decreasing $[\text{Cu}^{2+}]$ in the cuvette. Lower $[\text{Cu}^{2+}]$ means less light absorbed → lower absorbance. Using this lower absorbance on the standard curve gives a concentration lower than the actual unknown.
sample_responses.commentary: Complete chain of reasoning from dilution to lower absorbance to lower concentration reading; matches SG.

sample_responses.response: The cuvette path length decreases, reducing absorbance.
sample_responses.commentary: Adding drops of water to the cuvette does not change the optical path length; only concentration changes.
